coredata.dc:description: The controlled synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has been a key scientific problem in the field of catalysis. In this study, chiral CDs from different carbon sources were prepared using the hydrothermal method with d-proline as the chiral source. The effects of the carbon source skeleton (conjugated and unconjugated) and functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino) on its structure, reproducibility, and asymmetric catalytic performance in Aldol condensation were investigated. It was observed that hydroxyl groups in the carbon source are more favorable for enhancing the asymmetric catalytic activity and reproducibility of CDs compared to amino and carboxyl groups. The mechanism for the difference in reproducibility and asymmetric catalytic properties is investigated, which is ascribed to the effect of bonding (Directional factor or Random factor) between active sites and carbon cores, as well as the effect of carbonization (Random factor). The directional factor contributes to high reproducibility and asymmetric catalytic activity.

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of chiral carbon dots with high asymmetric catalytic properties and reproducibility for direct aldol reactions: The impact of functional groups of carbon sources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The controlled synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has been a key scientific problem in the field of catalysis. In this study, chiral CDs from different carbon sources were prepared using the hydrothermal method with d-proline as the chiral source. The effects of the carbon source skeleton (conjugated and unconjugated) and functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino) on its structure, reproducibility, and asymmetric catalytic performance in Aldol condensation were investigated. It was observed that hydroxyl groups in the carbon source are more favorable for enhancing the asymmetric catalytic activity and reproducibility of CDs compared to amino and carboxyl groups. The mechanism for the difference in reproducibility and asymmetric catalytic properties is investigated, which is ascribed to the effect of bonding (Directional factor or Random factor) between active sites and carbon cores, as well as the effect of carbonization (Random factor). The directional factor contributes to high reproducibility and asymmetric catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: The concept of chirality is a fundamental and indispensable characteristic in the realm of nature. The enantiomers, which are chiral isomers of each other, possess identical chemical formulas and exhibit similar structural formulas; however, they exert distinct effects due to their differing optical activities [1]. The demand for chiral compounds is steadily increasing in the field of fine chemicals, encompassing drugs, agrochemicals, cosmetics, and fragrances [2]. Currently, there are three main strategies for the synthesis of chiral pure compounds: chiral source synthesis, chiral isomer separation, and chiral catalysis. The process of chiral catalysis involves the transfer of chiral information from enantiomeric catalysts to non-chiral compounds through catalytic reactions, utilizing chirality-containing catalysts to generate corresponding chiral products from substrates lacking inherent chirality. This approach is considered the most cost-effective and practical method for large-scale production of chiral compounds. The development of efficient chiral catalysts has thus become the primary focus of numerous research endeavors [3]. In the past two decades, asymmetric organocatalysis employing chiral small molecules as catalysts has emerged as a rapidly advancing third fundamental pillar in the realm of chiral catalysis, following enzyme and metal-based catalysts [4]. In October 2021, Benjamin List and David MacMillian were recognized for their significant discoveries of enamine and imine ion-mediated organocatalysis and awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry [5]. The catalytic performance and enantioselectivity of organic small molecule catalysts are high [6–8]. However, as homogeneous catalysts, they encounter challenges in terms of recovery from the reaction solution [9].

Heterogeneous chiral catalysts have the advantage of easy separation from reaction solution, and is beneficial to the industrial production of asymmetric synthesis [10–12]. The surface of heterogeneous catalysts generally lacks intrinsic chirality, but chiral sites can be introduced by modifying the surface with chiral molecules. The modification methods include physical adsorption [12–14], encapsulation [15], electrostatic interaction [16], or chemical bonding [17,18]. The first three methods, which are non-bonding methods, result in weak interactions between small chiral molecules and the supports, leading to easy detachment of the chiral molecules. This is not conducive to catalyst recovery. In contrast, chemical bonding modification involves covalent attachment of small chiral molecules to supports, ensuring stable interactions between them. The covalent modification of heterogeneous chiral catalysts, however, typically involves multiple steps, leading to intricate procedures and diminished catalytic activity [12,17,18].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm. Due to a series of excellent properties such as strong quantum limiting effect and photoluminescence, CDs have attracted wide attention from researchers in many fields such as in optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, catalysis, agriculture, sensors, functional materials, and drug delivery, since they were first discovered in 2004 [19–22]. The CDs are composed of carbon core that are surrounded by shell containing surface functional group, and the surface functional group and carbon core are connected by covalent bond. Carbon dots have memory effect and structural tunability, and can inherit the properties of small molecule precursors, and the surface functional groups change with the change of precursors [23,24]. By hydrothermal method, the surface of CDs can covalently bind chiral functional groups in one step, which has the virtues of facile operation, uniform sizes, and abundant carbon sources [25,26]. The attachment of chiral molecules or functional groups to their surface gives CDs chiral characteristics. Chiral CDs have received a lot of attention from researchers for their superior biocompatibility, optical properties, and non-toxic and non-hazardous nature [27], which broaden their scope of applications in chiral recognition, asymmetric catalysis, and chiral detection [28–30]. The unique easy modification and nanoscale characteristics of chiral carbon dots enable them to combine the advantages of homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts, bringing new ideas for the development of novel and efficient chiral catalysts.

In 2020, Filippini et al. used amino acid-based chiral CDs to catalyze the asymmetric aldol reaction with a yield of 59 % and an ee value of 38 %, where the chiral CDs were one-step prepared under microwave at 240 °C for 3 min by employing precursors of (R,R)-or (S,S)-1,2-cyclohexanediamine in combination with l-arginine [31]. In 2021, our group prepared a chiral CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using d-proline (D-Pro) and citric acid as precursors, which obtained the yield of 97 % and the ee value of 73 % for aldol reaction [32]. Furthermore, the chiral CDs can be reused four times. More recently, Bortolami et al. [33] used one-step electrochemical methods to prepare chiral carbon dots to catalyze aldol reaction, the obtained ee value is 34 %. Although CDs have the advantages of simple preparation and stable structure, they face the problem of lack of controllable synthetic routes [25], low reproducibility and low asymmetric catalytic activity [34]. In addition, the influence of precursors (i.e., carbon sources and chiral sources) on the structure and asymmetric catalytic activity of chiral CDs remains unclear [35]. The elucidation of the influence of the precursor is helpful to control the synthesis of chiral CDs, enhance reproducibility of CDs preparation, and improve the asymmetric catalytic performance of chiral CDs.

The carbon source of CDs typically comprises both conjugated and non-conjugated carbon skeletons [36], along with functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups [37]. Asymmetric aldol reactions represent one of the most powerful method for constructing carbon–carbon bonds in an enantioselective fashion [38]. In this paper, chiral CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using D-Pro and various carbon sources as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The impact of the carbon source skeleton (conjugated and unconjugated) and functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino) on both the structure and asymmetric catalytic performance in direct aldol condensation was investigated.

Chiral CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using D-Pro and different carbon source as precursors. Typically, 0.5757 g (5.0 mmol) of D-Pro and 0.2752 g (2.5 mmol) of hydroquinone were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and uniformly dispersed by ultrasonication for 20 min. It was then transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel reaction vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180 °C for 2 h (or 4 h) in an oven. After that, a 0.45 μm water-based needle filter was used to remove the large particles and impurities synthesized by the reaction, which was then dialyzed for 72 h by using a 0.5–1 kDa fibre dialysis membrane, and finally the water was removed by lyophilization to obtain chiral CDs. The obtained chiral CDs were named as hydroquinone-CDs-2h (or hydroquinone-CDs-4h).

Other CDs were prepared using the similar method with the difference in the type of carbon source, including different functional group, and conjugated or non-conjugated structures. The selected carbon source covers the conjugated small molecules (p-phenylenediamine, terephthalic acid, phenol, or m-benzenetriol), non-conjugated small molecules (ethylene glycol, ethylenediamine, and oxalic acid). The obtained CDs are named as carbon source-CDs-2h (or carbon source-CDs-4h).

For comparison, CDs without carbon source are also prepared using similar method at hydrothermal time of 2 h. The obtained CDs are named as Pro-CDs-b. Besides, CDs without chiral source are also prepared using similar method at hydrothermal time of 2 h. The obtained CDs are named as carbon source-CDs-b, in which the carbon sources involve hydroquinone, p-phenylenediamine, and terephthalic acid.

The TEM images of carbon dots synthesized by D-Pro and conjugated molecules are shown in Fig. 1 . The average particle size of the synthesized carbon dots is 2.5–10 nm. Hydroquinone-CDs-2h (Fig. 1a) shows amorphous structure with no lattice fringes, while hydroquinone-CDs-4h (Fig. 1b) shows lattice fringes with a spacing of 2.6 Å, which is consistent with the (110) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. p-phenylenediamine-CDs (Fig. 1c and d) shows the lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 2.1 Å both in hydrothermal 2 h and 4 h, which is consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. Terephthalic acid-CDs (Fig. 1e and f) has the 2.6 Å lattice spacing for 2 h of hydrothermal time, and 2.1 Å of lattice spacing for 4 h of hydrothermal time. This indicates that for conjugated carbon source derived chiral carbon dots, amino and carboxyl groups are more conducive to the graphitization of carbon dots than hydroxy group.

For comparison, the TEM images of the carbon dots prepared from non-conjugated carbon sources are also determined (Fig. 2 ). Ethylene glycol-CDs-2h (Fig. 2a) and ethylene diamine-CDs-2h (Fig. 2c) are amorphous and do not show obvious lattice fringes, while for ethylene glycol-CDs-4h (Fig. 2b) and ethylene diamine-CDs-4 h (Fig. 2d), the degree of carbonization increases and lattice fringes with a spacing of 2.6 Å are observed, corresponding to the (110) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. This indicates that the carbonization degree of ethylene glycol-CDs is similar with that of its corresponding conjugated hydroquinone-CDs, while the carbonization degree of ethylene diamine-CDs is less than that of its corresponding conjugated phenylenediamine-CDs. Although, similar as its corresponding conjugated terephthalic acid-CDs, oxalic acid-CD-2h (Fig. 2e) and oxalic acid-CD-4h (Fig. 2f) respectively show 2.1 Å lattice spacing of (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon and 3.4 Å lattice spacing of (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, the lattice fringes of the latter are not clearly. These results indicate that conjugated carbon sources are more conducive to the graphitization of carbon dots than non-conjugated carbon sources. That is, the graphitization rate of carbon dots from conjugated carbon sources is higher than that from non-conjugated carbon sources [39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR)
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 ) exhibits the functional groups on the surface of carbon cores. For the case of hydroquinone-CDs-2h (Fig. 3a), the broad peak at 3372 cm−1 is assigned to adsorbed H2O or O–H stretching vibration originating from the carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups. The small dump at 3072 cm−1 may be caused by the N–H stretching vibration. The absorption signals at 2998 cm−1 and 2897 cm−1 is attributed to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of C–H. The absorption signals at 1613 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1 is respectively attributed to CO and C–O stretching vibration peaks, demonstrating the presence of COOH. The peaks at 1458 cm−1 is assigned to the vibration of the benzene ring skeleton, which may be derived from hydroquinone small molecules chemically adsorbed on the surface of the carbon cores or the carbon cores itself, because the carbon cores is a sp2 hybrid graphitic carbon, which also has a benzene ring structure. The adsorption signals in the range of 1060–1089 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–O–C peaks originating from anhydrides or esters, as well as the stretching vibrations of C–N originating from amides. This indicates that hydroquinone-CDs-2h have functional groups of amine, hydroxyl, carboxyl, benzene ring skeleton, and anhydrides/esters/amides. Other carbon dots display similar FT-IR spectra, indicating the existence of similar functional groups on the surface of carbon cores (Fig. 3b–e). The functional groups of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl may inherit from the corresponding raw materials. The 1H NMR of CDs also demonstrates that CDs inherit the functional group of the raw materials of the D-Pro (Fig. S1). However, in comparison to the raw materials, all carbon dots exhibit two additional peaks attributed to the benzene ring framework and anhydrides/esters/amides, regardless of whether they are derived from a single raw material or a combination of both carbon source and chiral source, as well as whether the carbon source is conjugated or unconjugated. The benzene ring framework may be assigned to the carbon cores, but not the conjugated carbon functional groups on its surface, because the carbon dots from un-conjugated carbon source also display the signal of benzene ring framework at 1427 cm−1 (Fig. 3e). The functional groups of anhydrides/esters/amides demonstrate that there is chemical reaction between carbon cores and raw materials in the process of forming carbons. The chemical reactions involved may be dehydration between carboxyl groups to form anhydrides, esterification between carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups to form ester groups, or amidation between carboxyl groups and amino groups to form amide groups. FT-IR analysis indicates that there are both inherited functional groups and new functional groups on the surface of carbon dots.

XPS spectra was measured to analyze the functional groups on the carbon dots surface. Fig. 4 reveals the XPS spectra of three elements: C (1s), N (1s) and O (1s). The deconvolution spectrum of C1s for hydroquinone-CDs-2h (Fig. 4a) displays five peaks at 284.2, 285.1, 285.6, 287.2, 287.7 eV, which are attributed to CC, C–C, C–N, C–O and CO. In the N1s spectrum of hydroquinone-CDs-2h, three peaks are corresponding to pyridinic N (398.6 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) and N–H (402.0 eV). The content of pyrrolic N is the highest, followed by pyridinic N, and the content of N–H is the lowest. The two fitted peaks at 530.2 eV and 531.2 eV in the O1s spectrum were assigned to CO and C–O/O–H groups, respectively. The C1s bands of p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h (Fig. 4b) and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h (Fig. 4c) are similar with that of hydroquinone-CDs-2h, revealing five peaks with similar binding energy. However, the relative contents of the carbon bounded to oxygen (C–O and CO) for p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h are lower than that of hydroquinone-CDs-2h and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h, which is related to the lack of oxygen in its carbon source. The N1s spectrum of p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h (Fig. 4b) and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h (Fig. 4c) are also similar with that of hydroquinone-CDs-2h, revealing three peaks with similar binding energy. However, the relative contents of the pyridinic N for phenylenediamine-CDs-2h are far higher than that of hydroquinone-CDs-2h and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h, which is related to the high amount of nitrogen in its carbon source. In the process of forming carbon dots, the carbon source of p-phenylenediamine will undergo dehydration and dehydrogenation, and the dehydrogenation process will produce pyridine nitrogen [41]. The O1s spectra of p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h (Fig. 4b) and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h (Fig. 4c) also display the existence of CO and C–O/O–H. However, the binding energy of CO and C–O/O–H are different for the three carbon dots. The difference of binding energy indicates the existence of different oxygen-containing compounds on the surface of carbon dots. The difference of oxygen-containing compounds may be related to the difference of oxygen-containing functional groups on the carbon source and the different chemical reactions of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. The XPS results are consistent with the results of the FT-IR spectra.

The circular dichroism spectra of carbon dots from different carbon sources are shown in Fig. 5 . Almost all the spectra display two peaks, and the peaks locate at similar positions, which is irrespective of the structure of carbon source. The circular dichroism signal peaks of carbon dots demonstrate that the synthesized carbon dots have chirality [42,43], which indicates that the choice of carbon source has little effect on the chirality of the carbon dots. Taking conjugated carbon sources containing –OH group as an example (Fig. 5a), a chiral signal peak with a stronger intensity, sharper peak shape and larger area appears in the wavelength range of 210–220 nm. The peak is derived from the chiral structure of the D-Pro moiety attached to the carbon dot surface [32], indicating that the chiral signal of the chiral source D-Pro is well preserved. The other chiral signal peak with a broader breadth and smaller area emerges within the wavelength range of 250–300 nm, potentially attributed to the π-π* transitions originating from sp2-hybridized carbon in the carbon core of carbon dots [43]. In addition, although the shape and location of the circular dichroism peaks are similar, there are small differences in the specific location and intensity, which may be related to different types of carbon sources.

It is also found that hydrothermal time affects the shape, position, and intensity of the peaks. As the hydrothermal time increased from 2 h to 4 h, the strong peak not only exhibits a decrease in signal intensity, but also undergoes a slight shift from its original position. For example, the strong peak shifts from approximately 215 nm in the case of hydroquinone-CDs-2h to 220 nm in the case of hydroquinone-CDs-4h. Simultaneously, there is a reduction of about one-third in the intensity of the strong chiral peak, while the intensity of the other weak chiral signal peak within the conjugated domain increases to some extent (Fig. 5a). This phenomenon may be due to be the fact that as the degree of carbonization deepens, the chiral groups outside the carbon dots gradually merge into the carbon core region, causing the intensity of chiral signal peak derived from the surface chiral functional groups to decrease, while the chiral signal intensity of the carbon core part is enhanced by the π-π* transition. These results indicate that hydrothermal time affects the chiral property of CDs including both its surface and carbon core, and the surface chirality of the CDs decreases with the increase of hydrothermal time, while the change of carbon core is vice versa.

The chiral carbon dots, prepared through hydrothermal treatment using D-Pro and various carbon sources, were employed as chiral catalysts in the direct aldol reaction between cyclohexanone and p-nitrobenzaldehyde. The catalytic performance of these chiral carbon dot catalysts is presented in Table 1 . The characterization of the aldol products are shown in supporting information (Figs. S2 and S3).

It is found that the functional groups of carbon source affect the catalytic performance of carbon dots. In the case of conjugated carbon source (entries 1, 6 and 7), hydroquinone-CDs-2h shows the highest asymmetric catalytic activity. The product yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone reaches 99 % when the reaction time is 18 h, while the reaction time using p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h as catalysts is 24 h. Terephthalic acid-CDs-2h shows the highest enantioselectivity with ee value of 83 %, next is hydroquinone-CDs-2h with ee value of 72 %, and p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h displays the lowest ee value of 38 %. The results show that the hydroxyl functional group of the conjugated carbon source have the strongest catalytic effect on the acceleration of the reaction, the carboxyl group have the strongest catalytic effect on the enantioselectivity, and the amino group shows weak catalytic effect and enantioselectivity. The carbon dots from un-conjugated carbon source containing hydroxyl group also show the highest asymmetric catalytic performance. Ethylene glycol-CDs-4h obtains 99 % yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone when reaction time is 18 h, and the ee value is 65 % (Entry 9). Ethylenediamine-CDs-4h produce only 34 % yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone even if the reaction time is extended to 48 h (Entry 11). For oxalic acid-CDs-4h, although the yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone could reach 96 %, the ee value is only 44 % (Entry 13), which is far lower than that of ethylene glycol-CDs-4h (Entry 9). These results indicate that compared with amino and carboxyl groups, the hydroxyl groups of carbon source are more favorable to the asymmetric catalytic activity of the carbon dots catalysts, whether carbon sources are conjugated or un-conjugated. The asymmetric catalytic activity of hydroquinone-CDs-2h also surpasses that of our previously prepared carbon dots using D-Pro and citric acid as precursors (Table S1, entry 2) [32], as well as other carbon dots synthesized with different precursors and methods (Table S1, entries 3 and 4) [31,33]. These reported carbon dots require a reaction time of 24 h to reach 99 % yield, but hydroquinone-CDs-2h by this work only takes 18 h. Furthermore, except lower than the catalyst of l-proline/hydrogel (Table S1, entry 5), the TOF of hydroquinone-CDs-2h is higher than other supported heterogeneous chiral catalysts (Table S1, entries 6–14). However, although the slightly lower TOF, the 72 % ee value by hydroquinone-CDs-2h is far higher than 18 % ee value by l-proline/hydrogel.

Besides, the asymmetric catalytic activity of carbon dots using single D-Pro as precursor without any carbon sources, that is, the Pro-CDs-b is tested (Entry 14). The yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone is 54 % at reaction time of 48 h using Pro-CDs-b as a catalyst, which is lower than that of hydroquinone-CDs-2h (Entry 1), p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h (Entry 6), and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h (Entry 7). This low catalytic activity may be related to the low number of carbon dots formed by a single chiral source. Carbon dots using single carbon sources as precursor without D-Pro don't show any catalytic activity (Entries 15–17), which is consistent with the none catalytic activity of their corresponding precursors (Entries 18–20). These results indicate that the carbon dots formed by the coexistence of carbon source and chiral source have higher catalytic activity.

In addition, hydroquinone-CDs-2h are also applied to the Aldol condensation reaction of other substrates, and the catalytic results obtained are shown in Table 2 . In the case of cyclohexanone as the substrate for ketones, the reaction rate depends on the types of aldehydes. When the aldehyde is 3-nitrobenzaldehyde, the reaction rate is the highest and the yield of 2-[hydroxy (3-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone is 97 % at reaction time of 32 h, and the ee value is 75 % (Entry 1). Next is 4-chlorobenzaldehyde, the yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-chlorobenzaldehyde)methyl] cyclohexanone is 99 % when the reaction time is 72 h, and the ee value is 88 % (Entry 2). The last is 4-bromobenzaldehyde, the product yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-bromobenzaldehyde)methyl] cyclohexanone reaches 99 % when reaction time is 72 h, and the ee value is 54 % (Entry 3). When the cyclohexanone is replaced by cyclopentanone, the reaction speed is obviously accelerated. Only 4 h is needed to reach 99 % yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrobenzaldehyde)methyl] cyclopentanone, and the highest enantioselectivity with ee value of 98 % (Entry 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, reusability, and effect of hydrothermal time

ce:section.ce:para: It is generally accepted that the reproducibility of carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal method is relatively low, which is one of the main obstacles limiting its application [34]. In this paper, the same carbon source and chiral source were used to prepare five carbon dots at the same batch under the same conditions, and their reproducibility was tested. The results are shown in Table 3 , which shows that the chiral carbon dots prepared with hydroquinone as carbon source have high reproducibility. When hydroquinone-CDs-2h is used for reaction, the yield of all five carbon dots reaches 99 % at reaction time of 18 h, the range is zero, and the enantioselectivity is between 69 % and 75 %, the range is 6 % (Entry 1). The reproducibility of the carbon dots prepared with p-phenylenediamine as the carbon source is poor, the enantioselectivity of the p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h is between 22 % and 62 % and the range is 40 % (Entry 3). The reproducibility of the carbon dots prepared with terephthalic acid-CDs-2h as the carbon source is also poor, when terephthalic acid-CDs-2h is used as catalysts, the yield range is 34 % (Entry 5). This result shows that the carbon source of the carbon dots affects its reproducibility. The reproducibility of the carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal method varies according to the nature of the carbon source.

The reusability of hydroquinone-CDs-2h is tested. After each experiment, the hydroquinone-CDs-2h was separated from the reaction system by extraction, and then the reactant was added to the separated carbon dots for the next reaction. By this method, the hydroquinone-CDs-2h was used for 5 consecutive times (Table 1, entries 1–5). The activity of the first four times was similar, and the yield of the reaction for 18 h could reach 99 %, and the ee value was about 70 %. The activity of the fifth time decreased slightly, the yield decreased by 6 %, and the ee value decreased by 10 %. To evaluate the stability of catalyst, the structure of hydroquinone-CDs-2h after the fifth use are determined by FT-IR and TEM. FT-IR spectra of hydroquinone-CDs-2h after the fifth use display the characteristic peaks of similar functional groups as the fresh carbon dots, indicating that the functional groups of carbon dots are stable (Fig. S4). The TEM image of hydroquinone-CDs-2h after the fifth use is similar with the fresh one, which shows 0.21 nm lattice fringe, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon (Fig. S5). The structural characterization and the reused results indicate that hydroquinone-CDs-2h has high reusability. The activity of carbon dots decreased slightly on the fifth use, which may be related to two reasons. The first reason may be associated with the partial loss of carbon dots during separation from reaction system. The other reason may be related to the partial adsorption of reactants or products on the surface of carbon dots, which decrease the number of active sites.

Effect of hydrothermal time is tested and showed in Table 3. It is found that hydrothermal time affect the asymmetric catalytic activity and reproducibility of carbon dots derived from conjugated carbon source. For all the carbon dots derived from conjugated carbon source, enantioselectivity of carbon dots decreased with hydrothermal time. The mean ee value decreased from 72 % (Entry 1) for hydroquinone-CDs-2h to 62 % for hydroquinone-CDs-4h (Entry 2), from 38 % for p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h (Entry 3) to 28 % for p-phenylenediamine-CDs-4h (Entry 4), and from 83 % (Entry 5) for terephthalic acid-CDs-2h to 35 % for terephthalic acid-CDs-4h (Entry 6). Circular dichroism analysis indicates that the chirality of carbon dots is affected by hydrothermal time. The chirality of the carbon dots' surface decreases as the hydrothermal time increases, while the chirality of their carbon core increases with prolonged hydrothermal treatment. The decrease of the enantioselectivity with hydrothermal time indicates that the asymmetric catalytic activity of carbon dots is mainly related to the surface chirality of carbon dots, while the chirality of the carbon core has little effect on the asymmetric catalytic activity of carbon dots.

It is also found that the reproducibility of carbon dots decreased with hydrothermal time. For hydroquinone, the range of yield increased from 0 of 2 h hydrothermal time to 26 % of 4 h hydrothermal time, and the corresponding ee value range increased from 6 % to 32 % (Entries 1 and 2). Similar phenomenon is observed for carbon dots from the other two conjugated carbon source. For p-phenylenediamine, the yield range increased from 4 % of 2 h hydrothermal time to 61 % of 4 h hydrothermal time (Entries 3 and 4). And for terephthalic acid, the ee value range increased from 14 % of 2 h hydrothermal time to 33 % of 4 h hydrothermal time (Entries 5 and 6). The decrease in reproducibility was also reflected in the significant difference in the time to reach the complete reaction for the five parallel samples, with some samples having longer reaction time and others having shorter reaction time. For example, hydroquinone hydrothermal samples for 4 h (hydroquinone-CDs-4h), some samples reacted fast, which completely reacted at 12 h, faster than the 18 h reaction time of the 2 h hydrothermal sample (hydroquinone-CDs-2h), and some samples did not react completely for 48 h, and far slower than the 18 h reaction time of the 2 h hydrothermal sample. A similar phenomenon also occurred in the 4 h hydrothermal sample of terephthalic acid (terephthalic acid-CDs-4h).

The carbon dots derived from un-conjugated carbon source exhibit no discernible asymmetric catalytic activity after a hydrothermal time of 2 h (Entry 8, 10, 12), whereas a noticeable asymmetric catalytic activity is observed after a hydrothermal time of 4 h (Entry 9, 11, 13). These results indicate that hydrothermal time could increase the reaction rate of carbon dots derived from un-conjugated carbon source. The effect of hydrothermal time is consistent with the analysis by TEM and circular dichroism. TEM analysis indicates that hydrothermal time promotes the formation of carbon dots, which explain the reason why hydrothermal time increases the reaction rate. TEM analysis also indicates that conjugated carbon sources are more conducive to the graphitization of carbon dots than non-conjugated carbon sources. This explains why carbon dots from un-conjugated carbon source don't show any catalytic activity at hydrothermal time of 2 h, but apparent catalytic activity is observed at hydrothermal time of 2 h for carbon dots from conjugated carbon source. Therefore, the asymmetric catalytic activity of the carbon dots depends not only on the hydrothermal time, but also on the functional groups and conjugated or non-conjugated structures of the carbon source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of chiral carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The small molecule feedstocks typically undergo four stages of processes to form CDs including condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and surface passivation [39,44]. The initial step involves the condensation reactions of small organic molecule precursors, leading to the formation of intermediate chain compounds. Common examples of organic condensation reactions include dehydration [45], amidation [46–48], aldol condensation [49,50], Schiff base condensation [51–54], and radical reaction [55,56]. The intermediates can undergo further polymerization or aggregation, leading to the formation of polymer-like carbon dots (also known as carbon clusters), which can be achieved through covalent, non-covalent, or other types of interactions [57]. The polymers are subsequently subjected to carbonization at elevated temperatures, resulting in the formation of the carbon core of the carbon dots [58–60]. The residual precursors, in the end, function as surface passivating agents that can be tailored on the carbon dots' surface to enhance their catalytic activity. The mechanism of forming carbon dots of conjugated carbon source and the unit reactions in which D-Pro may be involved are shown in Fig. 6 , based on the structure and asymmetric catalytic performance of carbon dots. The hydrothermal process leads to the dehydration and condensation of the phenol group on one hydroquinone molecule with the benzene ring on another, resulting in the formation of a new six-membered ring structure when three adjacent hydroquinone molecules are connected by a single bond [45,61]. This structure is then used as a polymerization unit to grow continuously, forming a mixture of the polymer and the prototype carbon core, which is further cross-linked and carbonized to form the carbon core. There are a large number of –OH functional groups on the surface of the carbon core from hydroquinone, which react with D-Pro by the dehydration condensation reaction under hydrothermal conditions as shown in formula (1) and bond D-Pro to the surface of the carbon cores, forming the final carbon dots of hydroquinone-CDs-2 h with a surface rich in –OH and chiral D-Pro. The formation process of carbon core from p-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid is similar to that of hydroquinone. Two types of carbon cores with surface rich in –NH2 and –COOH are formed by deamination, dehydrogenation, and dehydration condensation, respectively [41,62,63]. The bond between D-Pro and these two carbon cores is different from hydroquinone. Among them, the carbon cores formed from p-phenylenediamine can undergo dehydration and deamination condensation reactions with both the –NH2 and –COOH in D-Pro as shown in formula (2) and (3), while the carbon cores formed from terephthalic acid can undergo dehydration condensation reactions with both the –NH2 and –COOH in D-Pro as shown in formula (4) and formula (5). This shows that, unlike hydroquinone, which can react only with the –COOH in D-Pro and leaves the –NH– behind, both p-phenylenediamine and terephthalic acid react not only with the carboxylic group of D-Pro, but also with the amine group. The reaction between amine group and carbon sources could decrease the catalytic activity of the amine group of carbon dots. Although aldol condensation reaction can be catalyzed by bases, acids, and enamines, the catalytic mechanism of the reaction involving D-Pro is enamine catalysis, that is, the active sites is the amine group of D-Pro [64]. The controlled experiments also demonstrate that the carbon source molecules and the directly formed carbon dots from pure carbon source molecules exhibit negligible catalytic activity (Table 1, entries 15–20), whereas the carbon dots derived from pure D-Pro display significant catalytic activity (Table 1, entry 14). The results of these controlled experiments also demonstrate that the amine group of D-Pro serves as the active site for carbon dots. This elucidates why the presence of a hydroxyl group in the carbon source promotes the asymmetric catalytic activity of chiral carbon dots with D-Pro as a chiral source, while amino and carboxyl groups hinder their asymmetric catalytic activity.

The catalytic performance of carbon dots may be related to two factors, which is schemed in Fig. 7 . One is related to the bond between chiral source and carbon cores, which could be directional or random. (i) If the bonding does not affect the active sites of the chiral source, it is the directional factor. The directional factor could result in high catalytic activity, such as hydroquinone-CDs-2h, in which the bond is between the carbon cores formed by hydroquinone and the carboxyl group of amino acids without affecting the amino group of the active site; (ii) If the bonding affects the active sites, it become a random factor and will decrease the activity, such as p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h, in which the interaction is between carbon cores formed by p-phenylenediamine/p-phenylenediamine with the amino group of the active sites; (iii) Reproducibility is reduced and the bonding is random, if two types of bonding are present. For the examples of p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h/Terephthalic acid-CDs-2h, there are the presence of two types of bonds between carboxyl groups and amino groups, and between carboxyl groups and carboxyl groups. Another factor is carbonization. The results of transmission electron microscopy and circular dichroism show that high temperature can promote the carbonization of carbon cores, and the carbonization has two functions, one is to promote the formation of more carbon dots, and the other is to promote the carbonization of surface functional groups, reducing the number and chirality of surface functional groups. The catalytic activity of carbon dots formed from non-conjugated carbon sources is not active at 2 h of hydrothermal activity, but is active at 4 h, which may be related to the first function of carbonization. The enantioselectivity of carbon dots formed from conjugated carbon sources decreases with the increase of hydrothermal time, which may be related to the decrease of chirality caused by the second function. With the extension of hydrothermal time, the two functions of carbonization may function simultaneously, and which function plays the main role may be random, resulting in low reproducibility. This explains why the activity of some carbon dots increases with the extension of hydrothermal time, while the activity of some carbon dots decreases. Therefore, the bonding factor is directional or random, while the carbonization factor is random. If the directional bond function is greater than the function of carbonization, the reproducibility is high, such as the conjugated carbon source derived carbon dots prepared in 2 h by hydrothermal method. If the random factor of carbonization is comparable to or stronger than the bonding factor, it will cause a decrease in reproducibility, activity and enantioselectivity, such as the conjugated carbon source derived carbon dots prepared in 4 h by hydrothermal method.

The chiral carbon dots were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using D-Pro as the chiral source and various carbon sources. Conjugated carbon sources are more likely to promote the formation of carbon dots. Compared with amino and carboxyl groups, the hydroxyl groups of carbon source are more favorable to the asymmetric catalytic activity of the carbon dots catalysts, whether carbon sources are conjugated or un-conjugated. Hydroquinone-CDs-2h shows the highest asymmetric catalytic activity and the highest reproducibility. The product yield of 2-[hydroxy (4-nitrophenyl)methyl] cyclohexanone reaches 99 % and the ee value is 72 % when the reaction time is 18 h, while the reaction rate using p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h and terephthalic acid-CDs-2h as catalysts is slow. Furthermore, hydroxyl groups of carbon source are also more conducible to the reproducibility of CDs. The yield and enantioselectivity range of the five parallel experiments for hydroquinone-CDs-2h are less than 6 %, while the reproducibility of the carbon dots obtained from the other two carbon sources is worse, the enantioselectivity range of p-phenylenediamine-CDs-2h is up to 40 %, and the yield range of terephthalic acid-CDs-2h is up to 34 %. The effect of the functional groups of carbon sources on the asymmetric catalytic performance and reproducibility of chiral carbon dots is ascribed to two factors, one is the bonding between the chiral source and carbon cores, and the other is the carbonization. The bonding could be directional or random, while the carbonization is random. If the orientation factor plays a major role, the obtained carbon dots are highly active and highly reproducible, and conversely, the obtained carbon dots are low active and low reproducible. This work provides an effective method not only for controllable preparation of CDs but also for producing heterogeneous chiral catalysts with high asymmetric catalytic performance.

Jing Luo: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Shuaibin Wang: Writing – original draft. Yaxin Ding: Data curation. Ji Shen: Data curation. Chunli Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.
